title: Japanese Onigiri with Tuna

ingredients.name: short-grain rice or sushi rice
visual_checks: sona masoori is a good Indian substitute; avoid basmati as it won't bind
ingredients.notes: sona masoori is a good Indian substitute; avoid basmati as it won't bind

subs.name: sona masoori cooked slightly wetter than usual — it clumps well once warm

ingredients.name: canned tuna or salmon, drained well

subs.name: mashed avocado with a drop of soy sauce for a vegan filling
subs.notes: changes the diet type to vegan

subs.name: a few drops of any neutral oil plus a tiny drop of toasted sesame seeds

ingredients.name: nori sheets, cut into strips
visual_checks: each strip wraps the base of one onigiri
ingredients.notes: each strip wraps the base of one onigiri

subs.name: thin strips of roasted papad or toasted nori-flavoured rice crackers for a makeshift wrap

ingredients.name: toasted sesame seeds
visual_checks: mixed into filling or pressed onto outside
ingredients.notes: mixed into filling or pressed onto outside

steps.text: Rinse the rice in cold water, changing it three or four times until the water runs mostly clear. Drain, then cook with 500 ml water in a saucepan: bring to a boil, stir once, cover tightly, reduce flame to the lowest setting and cook for 12 minutes. Remove from heat and let it steam, still covered, for 10 more minutes. Do not lift the lid.
While the rice cooks, break up the drained tuna with a fork, mix in the sesame oil, sesame seeds and a small pinch of salt. The filling should be lightly seasoned — the rice will also be salted.

Once the rice is cooked and just warm enough to handle, wet both palms with cold water and rub a generous pinch of salt across them. Scoop about three-quarters of a cup of rice onto one palm and press a small hollow in the centre.

Spoon 1–2 tablespoons of tuna filling into the hollow. Bring the rice up around the filling from all sides, then cup both palms around the mound and press firmly with moderate force — rotate the ball while squeezing to compact it evenly into a triangle or rounded shape. The onigiri should feel solid, not crumbly.

Wrap a strip of nori around the base and lower sides of each onigiri, pressing gently so it sticks to the warm rice. Serve within 4 hours at room temperature, or pack immediately into a lunchbox; refrigerating makes the rice hard.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → mashed avocado with a drop of soy sauce for a vegan filling (changes the diet type to vegan); seaweed → thin strips of roasted papad or toasted nori-flavoured rice crackers for a makeshift wrap; gingelly-oil → a few drops of any neutral oil plus a tiny drop of toasted sesame seeds (the oil adds fragrance); rice → sona masoori cooked slightly wetter than usual — it clumps well once warm (avoid basmati; it won't bind)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Cook rice in a pressure cooker with 2 cups water for 2 whistles — it comes out slightly sticky, which is ideal for shaping. Sona masoori rice binds well when warm; rinse it thoroughly. Nori sheets are available at metro supermarkets and online grocery stores in India. Canned tuna (Tata's Real Good Fish or similar) is sold at most D-Mart or Reliance Fresh outlets.
